coredata.dc:title: Determination of spermine and spermidine in meat with a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe and a combinational logic gate
coredata.dc:creator: Abbasi-Moayed, Samira
Bigdeli, Arafeh
Hormozi-Nezhad, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe is developed with a wide color variation for visual determination of spermine (SP) and spermidine (SD) in meat samples. The green emission provided from the combination of yellow emissive quantum dots and blue emissive carbon dots turns into pink when SP or SD are present. The results show that the developed sensor has good linearity in the range of 0.5–10 and 0.5–80 µM for SP and SD and suitable detection limits were achieved including 0.2 and 2.1 µM for SP and SD. The probe was highly selective in the presence of amino acids and other biogenic amines. RGB indices were extracted to build a combinational logic gate for visual and simultaneous detection of SP and SD. The dual functional logic gate was easy to design and convenient to operate. Finally, a portable sensor was fabricated for visual, rapid and on-site assessment of meat freshness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric nanoprobe
Visual detection
Biogenic amine
Spermine
Spermidine
Meat freshness
Logic gate system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence probe
Incorporation of the probe into agarose hydrogel
Calibration and sensitivity of the ratiometric probe
Ratiometric fluorescence probe in agarose hydrogel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Iran National Foundation Grant No. 99015661 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra and corresponding images of yellow emissive MPA-capped CdTe QDs, blue emissive CDs and their mixture. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Chemical structure of (a) SP, (b) SD, (c) sensing strategy based on the aggregation of QDs.
Size distribution of MPA-capped YQDs (a) and YQDs in the presence of (b) SP and (c) SD (40 µM).
Fluorescence spectra and their corresponding images of the dual emissive YQDs-BCDs nanoprobe in the presence of (a, b) SP (c, d) SD at different concentrations.

Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the nanoprobe (R/R0 R = I570/I450) and different concentrations of (a) SP within a concentration range of 0.5–10 µM and SD within a concentration range of 0.5–80 µM.

(a) The fluorescence spectra, (b) the corresponding images of the YQDs-BCDs nanoprobe in the presence of binary mixtures of SP and SD at different ratio (total analyte concentration was 10 µM) and (c) linear relationship between the ratiometric fluorescence response of the nanoprobe (R/R0 R = I570/I450) and different ratio of SP/SD.

(a) The ratiometric green channel (G/B) and red channel responses (R/B) of the dual emissive nanoprobe in the absence and presence of SP, SD and their mixture, (b) the truth table and (c) the corresponding logic gate scheme. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Ratiometric responses of the nanoprobe and their corresponding images taken in the presence of potential interferences species including (a) biogenic amines and (b) different amino acids which might coexist in meat samples (1 mM).
Determination of SP, SD and their mixture in chicken meat and mutton meat.

head.ce:title: Determination of spermine and spermidine in meat with a ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe and a combinational logic gate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 11155-9516, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 11155-9516, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 14588-89694, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 14588-89694, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemistry Department, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran 11155-9516, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe is developed with a wide color variation for visual determination of spermine (SP) and spermidine (SD) in meat samples. The green emission provided from the combination of yellow emissive quantum dots and blue emissive carbon dots turns into pink when SP or SD are present. The results show that the developed sensor has good linearity in the range of 0.5–10 and 0.5–80 µM for SP and SD and suitable detection limits were achieved including 0.2 and 2.1 µM for SP and SD. The probe was highly selective in the presence of amino acids and other biogenic amines. RGB indices were extracted to build a combinational logic gate for visual and simultaneous detection of SP and SD. The dual functional logic gate was easy to design and convenient to operate. Finally, a portable sensor was fabricated for visual, rapid and on-site assessment of meat freshness.

ce:section.ce:para: Control over food safety and quality has always been a major global health concern and is receiving more attention in recent years (Fang, Zhao, Warner, & Johnson, 2017; G. Liu, Lu, Huang, Li, & Xu, 2018; Schaude, Meindl, Fröhlich, Attard, & Mohr, 2017; Zhang et al., 2021). The quality of protein rich food products, especially meat, can be assessed by monitoring changes in their physical properties such as changes in the flavor/taste, texture and color of meat. However, since intrinsic properties of meat such as the amount of connective tissues, fat contents, and the presence of bones are diverse between different types of meat samples, the application of physical assessment methods are often limited (Arshad et al., 2018; Escobedo del Bosque et al., 2020). As an alternative, chemical markers can be used for investigating the quality of meat. Biogenic amines (BAs) exist in a variety of meat products, such as fish, chicken, mutton and beef and can be considered as quality indices of meat (Sanchis, Yusà, & Coscollà, 2017). Although fresh meat contains very low concentrations of BAs, the high concentration of BAs that is usually found in spoiled meat (as a result of amino acid decarboxylation), can be considered as a threat to human health (Fang et al., 2017; Mazzucco, Gosetti, Bobba, Marengo, Robotti, & Gennaro, 2010). The quality ranges of BAs including lower than 5 mg/Kg, between 5 and 20 mg/Kg, between 20 and 50 mg/Kg and above 50 mg/Kg indicate fresh, acceptable quality, poor quality and spoiled meat, respectively (Triki, Herrero, Jiménez-Colmenero, & Ruiz-Capillas, 2018). Among different BAs, spermine (SP) and spermidine (SD) are two important polyamine BAs with similar structures that can be used for investigating the quality of meat products (Silva & Glória, 2002; Triki et al., 2018). High levels of SP and SD in human body (more than 0.5 nmol/mL) may cause important health problems such as abnormal blood pressure, headaches and tachycardia/worsening asthma (Cohen, LUndgren, & Farrell, 1976; Del Rio et al., 2018). Therefore, the detection of SP and SD is of great importance and can contribute in assuring the safety of meat products (Chopra, Singh, Venugopalan, Singh, & Kaur, 2017; Papageorgiou, Lambropoulou, Morrison, Kłodzińska, Namieśnik, & Płotka-Wasylka, 2018).

Common analytical methods for the determination of BAs include high-performance liquid chromatography (Mazzucco et al., 2010; Sentellas, Núñez, & Saurina, 2016; Zhao et al., 2007), gas chromatography (Awan, Fleet, & Thomas, 2008), capillary electrophoresis and electrochemical methods (Ream, Suljak, Ewing, & Han, 2003). However, these methods are often incapable of immediate, real-time and on-site detection (El-Nour, Salam, Soliman, & Orabi, 2017; Önal, 2007). Recently, different colorimetric and fluorimetric sensors (Nair, Debnath, Das, Wakchaure, Ganguly, & Chatterjee, 2019) have been reported for the detection of SP and SD utilizing pyrylium compounds (García-Acosta et al., 2006), coumarin derivatives (Lee, Scopelliti, & Severin, 2011), organic nanoparticles (Chopra et al., 2017), polymer compounds (González-Ceballos et al., 2020) and modified plasmonic nanoparticles (El-Nour et al., 2017; Kuo et al., 2018; Lin, Wu, Li, Song, Guo, & Fu, 2016), in which visual optical signals allow rapid detection. Nevertheless, the fabrication of the recognition probes in these optical sensors is usually time-consuming and not economically efficient.

Colloidal semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) have attracted extensive attention in sensing and biosensing applications. The interesting photophysical properties of QDs such as their high quantum efficiency, size-tunable fluorescence, broad excitation spectra and narrow emission band has made them exciting fluorophores for the design of fluorimetric probes (Chen, Wang, Yin, Wang, Wang, & Xie, 2012; Cui, He, & Chen, 2015; Freeman & Willner, 2012). Recent research has shown that the combination of QDs (as dynamic fluorophores) with suitable reference fluorophores within a ratiometric design can promote the application of fluorimetric sensors by providing visual detection ability (Abbasi-Moayed, Golmohammadi, Bigdeli, & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2018; Fahimi-Kashani & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2020; Ghasemi & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2019; Wu, Hou, Xu, & Chen, 2016). Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials with their incredible characteristics including physicochemical and photochemical stability, simple and low cost synthesis, high resistance to photobleaching and chemically inert behavior, can be the right candidate for reference probes in ratiometric fluoresce assays (Abbasi-Moayed, Golmohammadi, & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2018; Chu, Wang, Du, Yang, Yang, & Jiang, 2020; Molaei, 2020; Nejad & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2017; Tajik et al., 2020).

An important issue in the design of ratiometric fluorescence probes that needs to be addressed is the type of variation in the emission profile of the dynamic fluorophore in the presence of analyte(s) (Cao et al., 2020). If only quenching happens, in order to have a wide color variation in the response profile, the two fluorophore should have the maximum distance possible between their emission bands (i.e. emission colors) (Ma, Sun, Zhang, & Wang, 2015; Zhou et al., 2016). But if quenching is accompanied with spectral shift to longer or shorter wavelengths, the fluorescence emission band of the dynamic fluorophore should be close to the reference emission band (Bigdeli et al., 2019; Ghasemi, Hormozi-Nezhad, & Mahmoudi, 2018). The minimum distance between the emission bands in the latter ensures a wide response range and subsequently, a wide color variation profile. In this study, our aim was to provide a wide-ranging color tonality for visual determination of SP and SD via a ratiomeric design.

Mercaptopropionic acid (MPA), cadmium chloride dihydrate (CdCl2·2H2O), tellurium powder (Te) sodium borohydride (NaBH4), citric acid, urea, sodium dihydrogen phosphate and disodium hydrogen phosphate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The analytical standards of BAs including spermine (SP), spermidine (SD), histamine (HS), tyramine (TR), tryptamine (TP) and amino acids were purchased from Alfa-Aesar (USA). The stock solutions of BAs were prepared in deionized water. Deionized water, with the resistivity of 18.2 MΩ, was utilized for all the solution preparations.

A Varian Cary Eclipse photoluminescence spectrometer equipped with a Xe lamp and a 1 cm path-length quartz cuvette were utilized for fluorescence spectra recording. Size distribution and ζ-potential measurements of the particles were performed on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (ZEN3600). Measurements of pH were done with a Metrohm instrument model No. 827 pH meter equipped with a glass electrode. All photographic images were taken by a smartphone camera (Samsung Note 8) from 96 well fluorescence microplates in the UV viewing cabinet-Bronze which the distance and angle of the smartphone’s camera (eyes) from the testing zone (microplate) were shown in Fig. S1.

MPA-capped CdTe QDs were prepared according to a previously reported approach with slight modifications (Liu et al., 2015). Briefly, 200 µL of MPA was added to 10 mL deionized water containing 0.290 M CdCl2·2H2O; and then the pH of solution was adjusted to 9.0 by NaOH solution. Te powder (0.76 mmol) was added to a solution containing 75 mL of 64 mM NaBH4 under argon flow at 50 ℃. After 20 mins, the pH-adjusted MPA-CdCl2 solution was added and the temperature was increased to 150 °C. MPA-capped CdTe QDs with different emissions were prepared and collected at different reaction times and were allowed to cool in ice bath. The synthesized yellow CdTe QDs showed an emission band at 570 nm.

Water soluble blue emissive CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal route based on a previously described method, with slight modifications (Niu, Gao, Lin, & Wu, 2013). 0.3 g citric acid and 0.3 g urea were dissolved in 10 mL deionized water. The above solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the importance of proper selection of fluorophores in the ratiometric assay, different combinations of QDs (green, yellow and red) and CDs were tested. Each of these emissive nanoparticles possess a characteristic emission color and as shown in Fig. S2, different ranges of color variation are expected for each pair of these ratiometric probes. BAs suppress the fluorescence intensity of the QDs and shift their maximum emission wavelength and thus, result in a variation in the emission color of the probe. The variation that occurred when green and red QDs were used was not as wide as when yellow QDs was used. Therefore, the dual emissive ratiometric probe was obtained by mixing YQDs and BCDs to benefit from their extensive redshift and the resulting wide color variation that was ideal for visual detection of SP/SD.

Briefly, 400 µL of YQDs (10 times diluted) and 500 µL of BCDs (1000 times diluted) were mixed together. In order to obtain the highest analytical responses in the detection of SP and SD, experimental variables that could influence the signal were optimized, including pH and time. To this aim, the fluorescence spectra of the QDs were recorded in the absence and presence of the target analytes in a pH range of 6.0–11.0. Once the optimum pH was found in the presence of solely QDs, the pH of the ratiomertic probe (QDs + CDs) was then optimized. The final volume of the solution containing the ratiometric probe and 500 µL buffer (optimized pH) was then adjusted to 5 mL with deionized water. The stability of the developed nanoprobe was tested over 24 h (Fig. S3). To optimize the response time of the probe toward SP and SD, the kinetic behavior of the system was monitored over a time period of 30 mins, and all analysis were done at 10 mins. Finally, 50 µL of different concentrations of SP or SD were added to the above solution and after 10 mins, the fluorescence spectra and the images of the solutions were recorded. Size distribution and ζ-potential of the prepared ratiometric probe were measured before and after the addition of 50 µL of SP or SD (4 mM).

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe towards SP and SD was evaluated. For this purpose, 400 µL of the prepared YQDs (10 times diluted), 500 µL of the synthesized CDs (1000 times diluted) and 500 µL of 0.1 M phosphate buffer at pH 8.0 were mixed together and deionized water was added to a final volume of 5 mL. Finally, 50 µL of different AAs (0.1 M) and BAs (0.1 M) were added to the above solution and after 10 mins, the fluorescence spectra and the images of the solutions were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Incorporation of the probe into agarose hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The agarose hydrogel was prepared by dissolving 0.2 g agarose in 15 mL deionized water at 90 °C. After cooling the solution down to 60 °C, 5 mL of the prepared ratiometric probe was added with rapid stirring for 5 mins. Subsequently, the mixture was moved to the tube caps and was kept still for 1 h to form the hydrogel. The tube caps containing the ratiometric nanoprobe doped into agarose hydrogels were used as portable kits for detecting the target analytes with naked-eye. Solutions containing SP and SD were added to the tubes, the caps were closed and the tubes were kept upside down for 10 mins to allow the analytes react with the probe in hydrogels. The tubes were fixed on a black sheet and placed in the UV cabinet which is shown in Fig. S1 and then the fluorescence emission from the tube cap was visually observed and recorded by a smartphone camera under the excitation of 365 nm to test the practicability of the probe.

A piece of fresh mutton meat (shoulder) and a piece of fresh chicken meat (breast) were purchased from the grocery store (Tehran, Iran) and placed in a sealed ice container. Nine tissue samples of each meat sample (1 g) were chopped and placed in zip-lock freezer bags. The pH of mutton meat and chicken meat were tested by pH paper and reported about 6. The chopped meat samples were transferred to 15 mL falcon tubes and then SP, SD or their mixture were added to the tubes, separately. After mixing the meat samples with target analytes, ethanol was added for extraction. The prepared samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 mins and were then filtered to collect the extracted analytes from the real sample matrix (Gao, Grant, & Lu, 2015). Then 50 µL of the prepared real sample solution was added to the ratiometric fluorescence probe and the fluorescence reading was performed at pH 8.0 after 10 mins.

For assessing the freshness of the mutton and chicken meat, 21 tube caps containing agarose hydrogel incorporated with the ratiometric fluorescence probe were prepared. Three of them were left aside in the environmental condition of the experiment (25 °C) for 15 h, as blank. Mutton meat and chicken meat were transferred to the rest of the tubes, the tube caps were then closed and without requiring to turn the tubes upside down, the analytes that were released in the headspace were allowed to react with the probe to change its emission properties. The fluorescence of three tubes containing mutton meat and three tubes containing chicken meat which were stored at 4 °C and were considered as fresh meat were compared with blank tubes. Three of the mutton and three of the chicken meat-added tubes were stored at 25 °C to allow meat spoilage to progress. After 8 and 15 h, the variations in the fluorescence emission of the probe were observed and recorded to evaluate the freshness of meat over time.

The main goal of this study was to introduce a logic-gate based fluorescence probe for visual detection of SP and SD. For this purpose, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed by mixing YQDs, as the analyte dependent fluorophore, with BCDs, as the reference fluorophore. Fig. 1 illustrates the fluorescence spectra of pure YQDs and pure BCDs in which sharp emission bands at 570 nm and 450 nm related to the emissions of YQDs and BCDs, respectively are observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The mixture of YQDs and BCDs shows two distinct emission bands at 570 nm and 450 nm, resulting in a green emission color. As schematically demonstrated in Fig. 2 , the presence of SP or SD induces the aggregation of QDs due to hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions between the MPA groups on the surface of QDs and the amine groups in SP and SD structures. The aggregation of QDs leads to quenching or redshift in their emission spectra which can be explained by the electronic coupling between neighboring QDs (Koole, Liljeroth, de Mello Donegá, Vanmaekelbergh, & Meijerink, 2006; J. Liu et al., 2012). The analyte-induced aggregation of QDs in the presence of SP and SD was investigated by DLS analysis. Fig. 3 presents the size distribution of the QDs in the absence and presence of BAs of interest. The results indicated that the average size of QDs was about 2.5 nm, prior to analyte addition. As can be seen, in the presence of SP and SD, the size distribution of QDs moved to greater diameters, verifying their aggregation. On the other hand, the zeta potential analysis of QDs demonstrates that MPA-capped QDs have a negative charge at −5.62 mV which changes to 2.07 and 0.78 in the presence of SP and SD, respectively. The above evidence confirms that QDs were aggregated in the presence of SP, with a higher aggregation amount compared to SD.

In order to obtain the highest analytical responses for SP and SD detection, the effect of pH of the developed probe and the measurement time were studied. Since previous studies had described the instability of CdTe QDs at acidic pH, we tested the probe at pH levels above 6.0 (Ghasemi et al., 2018; Mandal & Tamai, 2008). As demonstrated in Fig. S4, the maximum stability and highest analyte-induced variation in the probe’s emission were achieved at pH 8.0. As such, all analyses were performed in phosphate buffer (pH 8.0, 10 mM). Fig. S5 illustrates that the ratiometric fluorescence responses reached plateau within 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration and sensitivity of the ratiometric probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 b, d increase in the concentrations of SP (0.5–10.0 µM) or SD (0.5–80.0 µM) changes the emission color of the probe from strong green to yellow-green, yellow, orange, pink and to pale purple. These color variations in the emission of the nanoprobe are due to the detected red shift along with the quenching in the fluorescence spectra of the YQDs in the presence of 0.5–10.0 µM of SP and 0.5–80.0 µM of SD which is shown in Fig. 4a, c. The different intensities of the emerging orange or red emission colors of the QDs and their combination with the BCDs provided a wide color variation for visual determination of SP and SD. However, these color variations could not be achieved by using the red MPA-capped CdTe QDs and blue CDs. As shown in Fig. S6, in the presence of SP and SD, a red shift occurred in the fluorescence of RQDs but the range of color variation was narrow. The combination of green QDs and blue CDs was also not a good candidate for designing the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe because their emission bands were very close together and the color tonality was not achieved (Fig. S2) (Ghasemi et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2016). As discussed earlier, the fluorescence of YQDs quenched dramatically by increasing SP or SD while the fluorescence intensity of BCDs remained almost unchanged. Fig. 5 displays the change in the fluorescence intensity ratio of R/R0 in the presence of SP and SD in which R and R0 are the I570/I450 ratio before and after the addition of the analytes to the fluorescence probe. Since the I570/I450 ratio before analyte addition is greater than this ratio after analyte addition, the fluorescence response of the probe (R/R0) was reduced by increasing SP and SD concentration. Therefore, the nanoprobe responses (R/R0) were linear toward 0.5–10 µM SP and 0.5–80 µM SD with correlation coefficients of 0.9875 and 0.9882 for SP and SD, respectively. Moreover, the limits of detection (3σ/s) were calculated to be 0.2 µM for SP and 2.1 µM for SD.

The capability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe for the determination of binary mixtures of SP and SD was also evaluated. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence spectra of the YQDs-BCDs nanoprobe in the presence of binary mixtures of SP and SD at different ratios (total analyte concentration was 10 µM). The ratiometric responses were linearly correlated to the increasing SP ratio. The emission color of the nanosensor changed from green to light green, yellow, orange and pink with increasing SP ratio (Fig. 6b). Therefore, the visual identification of SP/SD ratio in mixture samples was successfully achieved.

In order to combine visual ratiometric fluorescence sensing with intelligent molecular computing, the ratiometric nanoprobe was applied as a visual logic gate analytical device for SP and SD sensing. Logic gate devices have recently been implemented in various environmental and biological sensing applications (Barnoy, Motiei, Tzror, Rahimipour, Popovtzer, & Fixler, 2019; Beiderman et al., 2020; Liao, Li, Yang, & Chen, 2019). They are capable of handling multiple analytes simultaneously, making a conclusion based on the measured optical/electrical signals and finally, yielding a single digital output represented by 0 or 1 (i.e., qualitative Yes or NO answer).

In this study, the combination of YQDs and BCDs were used as building block of the ratiometric fluorescence sensing logic gate which demonstrated green emission color in absence of SP or SD. The ratiometric RGB based fluorescence responses (R/B and G/B) towards SP and SD were exploited for the fabrication of a Boolean logic gate system by employing SP and SD as two chemical inputs. The emission images and the truth table of the logic gate are given in Fig. 7 . According to Fig. 7a, the dotted line shows the defined threshold for the output of the logic gate (“on” or “off”): if the ratiometric RGB-based fluorescence responses (R/B and G/B) towards SP and SD were more than 1.5 (threshold), the output of the ratiometric response is “on” and if the fluorescence responses were lower than 1.5, the output of the ratiometric response is “off”. Therefore, the first row in the truth table represents the Boolean expression of the sensor in the absence (logical level “0”) of both SP and SD (0,0), showing green emission color in ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Therefore, the output of green channel ratiometric response (G/B) was on for (0,0) state whereas the output of red channel ratiometric response (R/B) was off. In all other input combinations of the logic gate, (0,1), (1,0) and (1,1), the outputs of red channel (R/B) were 1. However, the visual outputs of green channel (G/B) were on just for SD and for both SP and SD. To conclude, the Boolean expression of the logic gate function demonstrated that the outputs of this system defined as “OR”-type logic gate for red channel and combination of “Not” and “OR” gate for green channel.

The selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe towards SP and SD was evaluated. The fluoresce spectra and the emission color of the nanoprobe remained almost unchanged in the presence of other BAs and some amino acids (AAs) that might be present in real food samples (Fig. S7, Fig. 8 ). The negligible fluorescence intensity ratio (R/R0) of the nanoprobe in the presence of probable interferences (1 mM) revealed the high selectivity of the developed probe towards SP and SD (Fig. 8). Moreover, the response of the probe was recorded in the presence of SP and SD coexisting with all AAs and all BAs. The last two columns in Fig. 8 confirmed that the sensor has reasonable selectivity towards SP and SD and can detect the target analytes in real samples. The significant fluorescence responses towards SP and SD was attributed to the polyamine structures of SP and SD which allow multiple hydrogen bonds or strong electrostatic interactions between MPA on the surface of QDs and SP and SD.

The performance of the probe was assessed in real samples to ensure its practical application. For this purpose, two types of fresh meat (chicken and mutton meat) containing proteinaceous content were selected as real samples in this investigation. The fluorescence spectra of the probe after the addition of real samples containing SP and SD are shown in Fig. S8. The response of the probe in the presence of mixtures of SP and SD in meat was also measured. The results of real sample analysis are summarized in Table 1 . The recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) of the nanoprobe towards SP, SD and their mixture in real meat samples was further calculated. The recovery values in the determination of SP, SD and their mixture in chicken meat samples were 82, 90 and 104, respectively. In mutton meat, the recoveries of the probe were 86, 78 and 116 for SP, SD and their mixture, respectively. The results validated the applicability of the ratiometric probe for the detection of SP and SD in meat samples and justified its potential for food safety assessment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence probe in agarose hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Constructing portable fluorescence sensors for on-site detection of SP and SD is beneficial to monitoring the safety and quality of food products. Agarose hydrogels possess a high loading capacity and a low background fluorescence which makes them perfect candidates for developing portable fluorescence probes. In order to use agarose hydrogel as a visual detection platform, the YQDs-BCDs ratiometric fluorescence probe was mixed into 1% agarose solution at 60 ͦC and the mixture was immediately transferred into a tube cap where it was allowed to form the sensory hydrogel. The results of SP and SD determination in agarose gel are reported in Fig. S9a. By increasing the concentration of SP or SD in the tubes, the emission color of the ratiometric probe changed from green to pink (Fig. S10a). The truth table of the logic gate based on the responses of the probe in agarose hydrogel is depicted in Fig. S9b. Similar to the logic gate shown in Fig. 7, the outputs of this system were also defined as “OR”-type logic gate for red channel and combination of “Not” and “OR” gate for green channel. The applicability of the fabricated portable fluorescence probe was further tested for monitoring the freshness of meat. The meat samples were separated into two groups and were kept at two different temperatures for 15 h. The storage temperatures of 4 ͦC or 25 ͦC were chosen to ensure fresh and spoiled meat samples, respectively. The fluorescence emission of the YQDs-BCDs doped agarose hydrogel changed to orange and pink after keeping mutton meat at 25 ͦC for 8 h and 15 h, respectively. In contrast, no considerable changes were observed for the meat samples which were stored at 4 ͦC (Fig. S10b). The results indicated that the developed nanoprobe can be applied for on-site monitoring of the quality of meat products. The results in Fig. S10b showed that the YQDs-BCDs doped agarose hydrogel change to blue in the presence of chicken meat after 15 h at 25 ͦC due to the completely quenched emission of YQDs. However, the emission color of the probe changed to pink that confirmed mutton meat spoiled slower than chicken meat.

Finally, the results of this study have been compared with similar detection methods in terms of the details of the probe, sensitivity, detection ranges, and the real sample (Table S1) (Bhamore, Murthy, & Kailasa, 2019; Kailasa, Desai, Baek, Nguyen, Rafique, & Park, 2019; Kuo et al., 2018; Tu, Sun, & Xu, 2016; Yuan et al., 2018). Although in some reports, high sensitivity and low detection limits have been achieved towards SP, they suffer from complex synthesis routes and other drawbacks that limit their practical application. Besides, the wide color variation of the ratiometric probe designed herein makes this probe an efficient assay for easy recognition of important BAs including SP and SD.

In summary, a dual emissive nanoprobe was proposed for the determination of SP and SD, as two important chemical indicators of the freshness of meat samples. In this ratiometric probe, YQDs and BCDs were employed as sensing and reference fluorophores, respectively. Upon the addition of the target analytes, the fluorescence emission of YQDs was quenched and redshifted, while the fluorescence of BCDs remained unchanged. Precise selection of the fluorophores allowed a wide-ranging variation in the color tonality of the probe which was highly beneficial for visual detection of SP and SD. The results demonstrated a good linear behavior, acceptable accuracy and selectivity, and suitable detection limits including 0.2 µM for SP and 2.1 µM for SD. Moreover, the ratiometric probe was utilized to construct a combinational logic gate fluorescence sensor. In order to construct a portable sensor, the fluorescent probe was placed in agarose hydrogel in tube caps. We are confident that this nanoprobe can be utilized as a simple, visual and efficient method for the investigation of the quality and freshness of meat samples, meeting the needs of meat providers and consumers.

Samira Abbasi-Moayed: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Software. Arafeh Bigdeli: Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Reza Hormozi-Nezhad: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Iran National Foundation Grant No. 99015661.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132459.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
